docs.title: RacerAPI Engineering Handbook
docs.text:
This documentation is the architecture contract for this backend.

If you follow this handbook, you get:

  • predictable code paths
  • testable business logic
  • safe module isolation
  • low-risk onboarding for new engineers

If you ignore this handbook, you will produce hidden coupling, flaky tests, and expensive refactors.

docs.title: System Summary
docs.text:
• Runtime: FastAPI
  • Architecture: modular monolith
  • Layers: API -> Service -> Repo -> DB
  • DI mechanism: FastAPI Depends only
  • State model: no global mutable state
  • Boundary model: no cross-module imports

docs.title: Non-Negotiable Rules
docs.text:
• API layer does validation and transport only.
  • Service layer owns business rules and orchestration.
  • Repo layer does persistence only.
  • A module may import itself and core/shared only.
  • No module imports another module directly.

docs.title: Read This In Order
docs.text:
1. Architecture overview
  2. Request flow
  3. Dependency injection
  4. Module boundaries
  5. Add new module guide
  6. Coding rules and review checklist

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install racerapi

docs.title: DI Standard
docs.text: Use FastAPI Depends only. No custom container, no global registries, no service locators.

docs.title: Dependency Chain Pattern
docs.text:
# src/racerapi/modules/users/deps.py

def get_user_repo(db: Session = Depends(get_db)) -> UserRepo:
    return UserRepo(db)


def get_user_service(repo: UserRepo = Depends(get_user_repo)) -> UserService:
    return UserService(repo)

This chain gives: - explicit construction graph - easy test overrides - deterministic runtime wiring

docs.title: Endpoint Injection Example
docs.text:
@router.get("/{user_id}")
def get_user(user_id: int, service: UserService = Depends(get_user_service)):
    return UserRead.model_validate(service.get_user(user_id))

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Put DI providers in each module deps.py.
  • Keep provider functions small and pure.
  • Reuse core.get_db for DB session ownership.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Instantiate repos/services directly in route functions.
  • Create module-level singleton services.
  • Hide dependencies in globals.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Writing service = UserService(UserRepo(SessionLocal())) in API handlers.
  • Passing FastAPI Request down into service layer.
  • Mixing DI and manual construction in same endpoint.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text:
In tests only, when overriding dependencies:

app.dependency_overrides[get_user_service] = lambda: FakeUserService()

Do not carry test wiring into production code.

docs.title: Module Isolation Model
docs.text:
A module is a business capability with internal files: - api.py - service.py - repo.py - schemas.py - models.py - deps.py

Each module owns its own behavior and data access rules.

docs.title: Allowed Imports
docs.text:
• module -> itself
  • module -> racerapi.core.*
  • module -> racerapi.shared.*

docs.title: Forbidden Imports
docs.text: • module -> another module (for example users -> auth)

docs.title: Example Layout
docs.text:
src/racerapi/modules/
  users/
  health/

docs.title: Example of Correct Internal Use
docs.text:
# users/service.py
from racerapi.modules.users.repo import UserRepo

docs.title: Example of Forbidden Coupling
docs.text:
# forbidden: users/service.py
from racerapi.modules.health.service import HealthService

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: Direct cross-module imports create hidden dependency graphs that eventually block independent extraction and safe refactoring.

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Communicate across modules via API boundaries or explicit shared abstractions in core/shared.
  • Keep module-local invariants inside service layer.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Import another module because “it is easy right now”.
  • Put domain logic in shared helpers.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Building a "shared business utils" file that contains domain behavior.
  • Copying repo queries between modules instead of creating module-owned queries.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Only for controlled migrations where temporary adapters exist with: - TODO + owner + removal date, - test coverage proving behavior, - architecture review approval.

docs.title: Boundary Rules
docs.text:
Allowed: - module -> itself - module -> core - module -> shared

Forbidden: - module -> another module

docs.title: Enforcement Intent
docs.text: This rule exists to keep domains independently maintainable and future-extractable.

docs.title: Consequences of Violation
docs.text:
• Hidden business coupling
  • Circular design pressure
  • Refactor blast radius grows
  • Teams block each other on unrelated changes

docs.title: Practical Example
docs.text:
Good:

from racerapi.modules.users.repo import UserRepo
from racerapi.core.exceptions import NotFoundError

Bad:

from racerapi.modules.health.service import HealthService

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Duplicate tiny logic if it avoids domain coupling.
  • Move generic, domain-neutral helpers to shared.
  • Move cross-cutting policy to core.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Centralize domain behavior in shared for convenience.
  • Read another module's tables/repo directly.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Importing another module's schemas because they “already exist”.
  • Reusing another module's repo query method.
  • Building orchestration logic in API layer to stitch two modules.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Only during planned strangler migrations with: - explicit temporary adapter, - defined end date, - tests proving no functional drift.

docs.title: What We Built
docs.text:
RacerAPI is a FastAPI modular monolith optimized for team scale before infrastructure scale.

Key decisions: - One deployable unit, many isolated domain modules. - Strict layer boundaries: API -> Service -> Repo -> DB. - FastAPI Depends as the only dependency wiring mechanism.

docs.title: Why Modular Monolith
docs.text:
We chose a modular monolith because current complexity is domain complexity, not distributed-systems complexity.

What this gives us now: - single-process local debugging - no network calls between business domains - lower cognitive load for new developers - simpler transactions and consistency

docs.title: Why Not Microservices Yet
docs.text:
Microservices add operational cost immediately: - service discovery - distributed tracing - retry/idempotency policy everywhere - per-service CI/CD and ownership overhead

Until module boundaries are stable and scaling pain is proven, microservices are architecture tax.

docs.title: Why Not Engine-Based Architecture
docs.text:
The old engine/pipeline approach hid business flow behind framework-like orchestration.

Problems it caused: - stale APIs and dead abstractions - unclear ownership of business rules - difficult onboarding because control flow was implicit

Current approach makes flow explicit in code everyone can read.

docs.title: Example: Explicit Flow in Code
docs.text:
# src/racerapi/modules/users/api.py
@router.post("", response_model=UserRead)
def create_user(payload: UserCreate, service: UserService = Depends(get_user_service)):
    user = service.create_user(payload)
    return UserRead.model_validate(user)

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep architecture boring and explicit.
  • Make business flow visible from route to service to repo.
  • Keep each module independently testable.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Add new framework layers (custom containers, decorators, lifecycle engines).
  • Introduce cross-module direct calls.
  • Hide logic in implicit middleware chains.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Treating modular monolith as a temporary toy and skipping boundaries.
  • Creating “utility” modules that become cross-domain backdoors.
  • Writing services that only pass through to repos with no business logic.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Break “single deployable unit” only when all are true: - one module has independent scaling profile for months, - module contracts are stable, - team ownership and SLO boundaries are already clear.

docs.title: Canonical Flow
docs.text:
Request -> Router -> Service -> Repo -> DB -> Response

This is mandatory. Any shortcut is architectural debt.

docs.title: Step-by-Step
docs.text:
1. Router parses HTTP input into schema objects.
  2. Router resolves service via Depends.
  3. Service validates business rules and orchestrates operations.
  4. Service calls repo for persistence/query.
  5. Repo executes SQLAlchemy operations.
  6. Service returns domain result.
  7. Router maps result to response schema.

docs.title: Real Code Walkthrough
docs.text:
# src/racerapi/modules/users/api.py
@router.patch("/{user_id}", response_model=UserRead)
def patch_user(
    user_id: int,
    payload: UserUpdate,
    service: UserService = Depends(get_user_service),
):
    user = service.update_user(user_id=user_id, payload=payload)
    return UserRead.model_validate(user)

# src/racerapi/modules/users/service.py
def update_user(self, user_id: int, payload: UserUpdate):
    user = self.get_user(user_id)
    if payload.full_name is not None:
        user.full_name = payload.full_name
    if payload.is_active is not None:
        user.is_active = payload.is_active
    return self.repo.update(user)

# src/racerapi/modules/users/repo.py
def update(self, entity: User) -> User:
    self.db.add(entity)
    self.db.commit()
    self.db.refresh(entity)
    return entity

docs.title: Text Diagram
docs.text:
HTTP PATCH /users/{id}
  |
  v
users.api.patch_user(...)
  |
  v
users.service.update_user(...)
  |
  v
users.repo.update(...)
  |
  v
SQLAlchemy Session -> Database
  |
  v
User ORM entity
  |
  v
UserRead response model
  |
  v
HTTP 200 JSON

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep transport concerns in API layer.
  • Keep business branching in service layer.
  • Keep DB session usage in repo layer only.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Call SQLAlchemy directly in route handlers.
  • Put business decisions inside repo methods.
  • Return raw ORM internals without schema mapping.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Performing uniqueness checks in routes.
  • Committing transactions from service methods.
  • Returning dict literals from service while other endpoints return schemas.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Rare read-only health checks may skip service logic if there is no business rule. Even then, keep repo for DB calls.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
API layer owns: - request/response schema validation - HTTP status mapping - dependency resolution via Depends

API layer must not own business decisions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@router.get("/{user_id}", response_model=UserRead)
def get_user(user_id: int, service: UserService = Depends(get_user_service)):
    user = service.get_user(user_id)
    return UserRead.model_validate(user)

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep handlers thin.
  • Convert domain result to API schema.
  • Use explicit status codes on write endpoints.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Execute SQLAlchemy in route handlers.
  • Embed business branching (if role == ...) in handlers.
  • Build ad-hoc JSON response contracts per route.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Catching all exceptions in route and returning 200 with error body.
  • Returning ORM objects directly.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Never for business endpoints. For very simple liveness checks, route can call a minimal service wrapper.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
Repo layer owns: - SQLAlchemy query and persistence operations - transaction commit/refresh for entity mutations

Repo layer does not own business decisions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def list_users(self, offset: int, limit: int) -> tuple[list[User], int]:
    items = self.db.execute(select(User).offset(offset).limit(limit)).scalars().all()
    total = self.db.execute(select(func.count()).select_from(User)).scalar_one()
    return items, total

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep methods query-focused and deterministic.
  • Return typed ORM/domain objects.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Raise HTTPException from repo.
  • Validate business policy in repo (if user is premium).
  • Import other module repos.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Putting pagination parameter validation in repo.
  • Returning mixed return types (dict in one method, ORM in another).

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Never for business policy. Keep repo pure persistence.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Schemas define external and internal data contracts: - request payload validation - response shaping - contract stability across endpoints

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class UserCreate(BaseModel):
    email: EmailStr
    full_name: str = Field(min_length=2, max_length=255)

class UserRead(BaseModel):
    id: int
    email: EmailStr
    full_name: str
    is_active: bool
    model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep API schemas in module schemas.py.
  • Use strict field constraints.
  • Separate create/update/read schemas.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Reuse DB ORM classes as API schemas.
  • Put business logic methods inside schemas.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• One giant schema used for create/update/read.
  • Optional fields in create payloads without explicit reason.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Temporary compatibility schemas are allowed during version migrations; add removal plan in PR.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text: Service layer owns: - business rules - orchestration across repo operations - domain error generation

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def get_user(self, user_id: int):
    user = self.repo.get_by_id(user_id)
    if user is None:
        raise NotFoundError(f"User {user_id} was not found")
    return user

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep service methods named by business intent (create_user, deactivate_user).
  • Validate business invariants here.
  • Raise domain exceptions, not HTTP errors.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Put HTTP request objects in service signatures.
  • Put SQL query composition in service.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Fake service layer that just forwards repo.method(...) with no rules.
  • Service committing raw transaction boundaries through direct DB session access.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Read-only pass-through methods are acceptable if the business rule is truly empty and likely to stay empty. Document this in code review.

docs.title: Current Model
docs.text:
Configuration is centralized in src/racerapi/core/config.py through pydantic-settings.

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    app_name: str = "RacerAPI"
    debug: bool = False
    database_url: str = "sqlite:///./racerapi.db"

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Add new settings fields in one place.
  • Use environment variable prefix for all runtime config.
  • Keep defaults safe for local dev only.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Scatter config reads across modules.
  • Read environment variables directly from business code.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Adding module-specific constants in service code instead of config.
  • Introducing mutable global config objects.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Never in production paths. Test-only overrides are acceptable via fixtures.

docs.title: Current Setup
docs.text:
• SQLAlchemy engine/session in src/racerapi/db/session.py
  • Declarative base in src/racerapi/db/base.py
  • Tables created on app startup lifespan

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
engine = create_engine(settings.database_url, future=True)
SessionLocal = sessionmaker(bind=engine, autoflush=False, autocommit=False, class_=Session)

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Access DB through repo layer only.
  • Keep one session lifecycle ownership path (get_db).
  • Use migrations for schema evolution in real environments.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Open new sessions manually in services/routes.
  • Share session globals across requests.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Committing inside loops in service code.
  • Creating engine in module-level code outside DB core package.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: One-off scripts may construct sessions directly, but never in request handlers.

docs.title: Root Dependencies
docs.text:
Core dependency providers live in src/racerapi/core/deps.py.

def get_db() -> Generator[Session, None, None]:
    db = SessionLocal()
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        db.close()

docs.title: Chained Dependencies
docs.text:
Module providers build on core providers:

def get_user_repo(db: Session = Depends(get_db)) -> UserRepo:
    return UserRepo(db)

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep providers side-effect free.
  • Keep dependency graph explicit and linear.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Hide service construction in startup hooks.
  • Use global object registries.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Creating providers that perform DB writes.
  • Injecting more dependencies than endpoint needs.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Complex graph factories are acceptable only when domain complexity requires them and the graph remains explicit in provider code.

docs.title: Strategy
docs.text:
• Services raise domain exceptions (NotFoundError, ConflictError, ValidationError).
  • App-level exception handlers map domain exceptions to HTTP responses.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@app.exception_handler(NotFoundError)
async def not_found_handler(_: Request, exc: NotFoundError):
    return JSONResponse(status_code=404, content={"detail": str(exc)})

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Raise domain-specific exceptions from service layer.
  • Keep HTTP status mapping centralized in app composition.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Raise HTTPException in repo.
  • Return error dicts with 200 status.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Catching broad exceptions and hiding root cause.
  • Duplicating exception-to-status mapping in every endpoint.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: For external SDK failures, you may map to domain exceptions at service boundary before re-raising.

docs.title: Debugging Order
docs.text:
1. Reproduce with failing test.
  2. Confirm layer where behavior diverges.
  3. Fix in correct layer only.
  4. Add regression test.

docs.title: Layer-Oriented Triage
docs.text:
• Wrong status code or response shape -> API layer
  • Wrong business decision -> Service layer
  • Wrong data returned/written -> Repo layer
  • Session/config issues -> Core/DB layer

docs.title: Example Workflow
docs.text:
pytest tests/test_users_service.py -q
pytest tests/test_users_api.py -q

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Start debugging from failing behavior, not assumptions.
  • Keep breakpoints near boundary transitions.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Patch symptom in API if root cause is service/repo.
  • Add broad try/except to silence failures.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Fixing test to match bug.
  • Introducing cross-module import during emergency patch.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Temporary hotfixes are allowed only with follow-up ticket and regression test in same change set.

docs.title: Start Server
docs.text:
python -m racerapi.main

or

uvicorn racerapi.main:app --reload

docs.title: Validate Endpoints
docs.text:
curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/health
curl "http://127.0.0.1:8000/users?page=1&page_size=10"

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Use --reload for local iteration only.
  • Verify docs and health endpoint after startup.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Run production with reload mode.
  • Treat startup table creation as replacement for migrations in prod.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Using wrong import path (app.main instead of racerapi.main).
  • Assuming DB is initialized without startup lifespan execution.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: For smoke tests, running module directly is acceptable if command environment is controlled.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • virtual environment
  • editable install

docs.title: Setup Commands
docs.text:
python -m venv .venv
.venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -e .
pip install -e .[dev]

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Use isolated virtual env per clone.
  • Keep dependencies pinned through project metadata.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Install random global packages and assume project works for others.
  • Skip -e install when developing package code.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Running tests without editable install and getting import errors.
  • Mixing multiple Python interpreters in one shell.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Containerized CI can install non-editable wheels; local dev should stay editable.

docs.title: Test Pyramid
docs.text:
• Unit tests: service logic (fast, isolated)
  • Integration tests: repo + in-memory DB
  • API tests: FastAPI TestClient

Current examples: - tests/test_users_service.py - tests/test_users_repo.py - tests/test_users_api.py

docs.title: Run Tests
docs.text: pytest -q

docs.title: Service Unit Test Example
docs.text:
with pytest.raises(ConflictError):
    service.create_user(UserCreate(email="john@example.com", full_name="John"))

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep service tests independent from DB.
  • Use in-memory SQLite for repo integration tests.
  • Test endpoint status and response contracts.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Mock everything in repo tests.
  • Assert implementation details instead of behavior.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Writing only API tests and skipping business-rule unit tests.
  • Sharing mutable test database state across test modules.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: End-to-end tests may use real DB containers for critical flows, but keep unit/integration suite as fast gate.

docs.title: Step-by-Step
docs.text:
1. Create folder: src/racerapi/modules/<module_name>/
  2. Add required files: api.py, service.py, repo.py, schemas.py, models.py, deps.py
  3. Add model to SQLAlchemy metadata import path.
  4. Implement DI providers in deps.py.
  5. Register router in src/racerapi/app/__init__.py.
  6. Add tests: service unit, repo integration, API tests.

docs.title: Full Example Skeleton
docs.text:
# modules/orders/deps.py

def get_order_repo(db: Session = Depends(get_db)) -> OrderRepo:
    return OrderRepo(db)


def get_order_service(repo: OrderRepo = Depends(get_order_repo)) -> OrderService:
    return OrderService(repo)

# modules/orders/api.py
router = APIRouter(prefix="/orders", tags=["orders"])

@router.get("/{order_id}", response_model=OrderRead)
def get_order(order_id: int, service: OrderService = Depends(get_order_service)):
    return OrderRead.model_validate(service.get_order(order_id))

# app/__init__.py
from racerapi.modules.orders.api import router as orders_router
app.include_router(orders_router)

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Start from a small read endpoint.
  • Add service business checks before write endpoints.
  • Keep module self-contained.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Reuse another module's repo to move faster.
  • Add endpoints before schemas are stable.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Forgetting to include router in app factory.
  • Missing dependency provider chain.
  • Putting business branching in route handlers.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: During emergency fixes you may implement a minimal read endpoint first, but follow with full layer separation in the same PR cycle.

docs.title: Required Files Per Module
docs.text: Each domain module must contain: - api.py - service.py - repo.py - schemas.py - models.py - deps.py

docs.title: Why This Structure
docs.text: It forces explicit ownership: - transport in API - rules in service - persistence in repo

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
src/racerapi/modules/users/
  api.py
  service.py
  repo.py
  schemas.py
  models.py
  deps.py

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep module files cohesive and small.
  • Keep module tests close to behavior intent.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Put shared app config inside module folders.
  • Add extra abstraction folders without clear value.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Creating managers, handlers, and processors with overlapping responsibility.
  • Using module __init__.py for runtime logic.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Add subpackages only when module complexity requires it, for example separate query/read model folders. Keep layer ownership unchanged.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: The users module demonstrates the production contract for all modules.

docs.title: API Layer
docs.text:
@router.post("", response_model=UserRead, status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED)
def create_user(payload: UserCreate, service: UserService = Depends(get_user_service)):
    user = service.create_user(payload)
    return UserRead.model_validate(user)

docs.title: Service Layer
docs.text:
def create_user(self, payload: UserCreate):
    existing = self.repo.get_by_email(payload.email)
    if existing is not None:
        raise ConflictError(f"User with email {payload.email} already exists")
    return self.repo.create(email=str(payload.email), full_name=payload.full_name)

docs.title: Repo Layer
docs.text:
def get_by_email(self, email: str) -> User | None:
    stmt = select(User).where(User.email == email)
    return self.db.execute(stmt).scalar_one_or_none()

docs.title: DI Wiring
docs.text:
def get_user_repo(db: Session = Depends(get_db)) -> UserRepo:
    return UserRepo(db)

def get_user_service(repo: UserRepo = Depends(get_user_repo)) -> UserService:
    return UserService(repo)

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Raise domain exceptions from service.
  • Keep repo methods query-focused.
  • Return schema models from API.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Raise HTTPException inside repo.
  • Parse request headers in service.
  • Commit DB transactions from API.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Duplicate uniqueness checks in both API and service.
  • Returning ORM directly without schema mapping policy.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Never bypass service for create/update/delete flows.

docs.title: 1) Business Logic in Routes
docs.text:
Bad:

@router.post("")
def create_user(payload: UserCreate, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    if db.execute(...):
        raise HTTPException(409, "duplicate")

Why dangerous: - duplicates service behavior - hard to unit test - spreads business policy across transport layer

docs.title: 2) Cross-Module Imports
docs.text:
Bad:

from racerapi.modules.auth.service import AuthService

Why dangerous: - hidden coupling - extraction cost multiplies

docs.title: 3) Global State
docs.text:
Bad:

CURRENT_USER_CACHE = {}

Why dangerous: - request leakage - concurrency bugs - non-deterministic tests

docs.title: 4) Fake Service Layer
docs.text:
Bad:

def create_user(self, payload):
    return self.repo.create(payload)

Why dangerous: - layer exists without value - business rules leak upward or downward

docs.title: 5) Over-Engineering
docs.text:
Bad signs: - custom DI container - custom routing DSL - custom lifecycle engine

Why dangerous: - framework drift - onboarding friction - stale abstractions

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Keep architecture explicit and boring.
  • Delete abstractions that do not reduce real complexity.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text: • Introduce “future-proofing” patterns with no current need.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: Never for global state and cross-module imports. Those are hard stops.

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
• Module folders: plural nouns (users, orders)
  • Files: fixed layer names (api.py, service.py, repo.py, schemas.py, models.py, deps.py)
  • Service methods: business-intent verbs (create_user, deactivate_user)
  • Repo methods: data operation names (get_by_id, list_users, update)

docs.title: File Structure Rules
docs.text:
• Keep one domain per module folder.
  • Keep API handlers short and deterministic.
  • Keep schema and ORM model separated.

docs.title: Functions vs Classes
docs.text:
• Use classes where stateful dependency is meaningful (UserService, UserRepo).
  • Use functions for providers and simple utilities.

docs.title: Error Handling Rules
docs.text:
• Raise domain exceptions in service layer.
  • Map to HTTP once in app-level handlers.

docs.title: DO
docs.text:
• Write explicit type hints on public methods.
  • Keep return types stable.
  • Make side effects obvious.

docs.title: DON'T
docs.text:
• Hide behavior in magic decorators.
  • Add custom framework layers around FastAPI.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Utility sprawl in shared with domain behavior.
  • Endpoint-specific one-off patterns that bypass conventions.

docs.title: When to Break the Rule
docs.text: A narrow exception is acceptable if it removes complexity and is documented in PR with trade-off rationale.

docs.title: PR Review Checklist
docs.text: Use this checklist in every backend PR.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
• [ ] Route logic stays in API layer only
  • [ ] Business rules are in service layer
  • [ ] Repo contains only DB operations
  • [ ] No cross-module imports
  • [ ] DI uses Depends providers, no manual wiring in routes

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text:
• [ ] New endpoints have request and response schemas
  • [ ] Exceptions are domain exceptions in service layer
  • [ ] API status code mapping is correct
  • [ ] No global mutable state introduced

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
• [ ] Service unit tests added/updated
  • [ ] Repo integration tests added/updated
  • [ ] API tests added/updated
  • [ ] Regression test exists for bug fixes

docs.title: Operational Readiness
docs.text:
• [ ] Config changes documented
  • [ ] Migration impact documented
  • [ ] Backward compatibility considered

docs.title: Common Reviewer Misses
docs.text:
• Verifying endpoint works but missing service-level invariant checks.
  • Accepting cross-module imports hidden in helper files.
  • Ignoring response schema drift.

docs.title: When to Approve Exceptions
docs.text: Only with explicit rationale, owner, and cleanup deadline in PR description.
